title: Token-bucket rate limiter with sliding-window fallback
summary: A distributed rate limiter for API gateways built on token bucket semantics, stored in Redis with local-process fallback. Supports per-user, per-IP, and per-API-key limits with burst allowance. Targets 50k+ gateway QPS with rate-check added latency <1ms p99.

phases.requirements: Functional: (1) allow or reject a request based on an identifier (userId / apiKey / IP) against a configured limit; (2) multiple limits per identifier (per-second, per-minute, per-hour, per-day); (3) support burst = capacity above steady rate; (4) admin UI to view current limits and violations. Non-functional: (a) rate-check adds <1ms p99 to gateway path; (b) graceful degradation — if Redis is down, fail open (with logging) rather than reject everything; (c) accurate to within 1% under typical load; (d) horizontally scalable to 50k+ QPS. Out of scope: per-endpoint quotas (handled upstream), tarpitting slow responses.
phases.capacityEstimation: Gateway QPS: 50k steady, 150k peak. Each request = 1 rate-check call to Redis. Redis ops: 150k ops/sec peak (well within single-cluster capacity). Memory: ~100 bytes per active bucket × 10M active identifiers = 1 GB + replicas. Configured limits: ~100k rules (per user/apiKey/IP tier × time window), stored in a config table + in-process LRU. Violations log: ~0.5% of traffic = 750/sec peak, streamed to analytics.
phases.apiDesign: Internal (SDK): rateLimiter.check(identifier, rule) → { allowed: boolean, remaining: number, resetAt: timestamp, retryAfter?: number }. Used by API gateway middleware before forwarding to upstream service. Admin HTTP: GET /admin/limits/{id} → current bucket state; POST /admin/limits/rules → set/update a rule; GET /admin/violations?since=X → recent violations. Rate-limit response headers on rejected requests: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, Retry-After.
phases.dataModel: Redis keys: `rl:{id}:{window}` storing a token bucket as a HASH { tokens: float, lastRefill: epoch_ms }. TTL = max configured window + buffer. Rule config table (Postgres): (rule_id, identifier_type, identifier_pattern, window, capacity, refill_rate, priority). Violations stream (Kafka → data warehouse): { identifier, rule_id, allowed, tokens_remaining, timestamp }. In-process LRU cache on the rate limiter client holds rules (TTL 60s) to avoid DB lookup on every check.
phases.architecture: API Gateway → Rate Limiter Client (SDK, in-process) → Redis (token bucket state) — with local-process fallback on Redis failure. Control plane: Admin API → Rules DB → Kafka topic `rate-limit-rules` → all Rate Limiter Clients subscribe to rule changes (push-based invalidation). Observability: Rate Limiter Client emits metrics (checks/sec, rejections/sec, Redis latency) and violations events. A background reconciler job rebuilds the LRU from Rules DB every 60s as a fallback for missed invalidations.
phases.deepDive: Token bucket algorithm: on check, compute `tokens = min(capacity, tokens_at_last_refill + (now - lastRefill) * refill_rate)`. If tokens >= cost, decrement and allow; else reject and return retryAfter. Implemented as a Lua script on Redis so the refill+check is atomic — two nodes checking the same bucket see a consistent state. Script pinned by SHA; deployed via redis-cli SCRIPT LOAD on startup. Sliding-window fallback: when a customer needs stricter accuracy than token bucket's burst tolerance, the Lua script switches to a count of (now - window) to now, using a sorted set — O(log n) per check but exact. Fail-open policy: if Redis times out (>5ms), the Rate Limiter Client allows the request, logs a warning, and flags the instance for degraded mode. Admin is paged if degradation exceeds 1% of checks for >30s.
phases.tradeoffs: Token bucket vs sliding window: bucket is O(1) memory and fast but allows bursts; sliding window is exact but slower and uses more memory. We default to bucket and expose sliding-window as an opt-in per rule. Centralised Redis vs local-only counter: centralised is the source of truth across gateway instances; local-only would be faster but wrong under multi-instance gateways. Fail-open vs fail-closed: we fail-open because rate limiting is a cost-control and abuse-mitigation feature, not a security boundary. For auth, a separate system fails closed.

componentAnnotations.purpose: Entry point; calls the rate limiter before forwarding
componentAnnotations.technology: Envoy or Nginx + custom WASM/Lua plugin
componentAnnotations.notes: Rate check on the hot path — every added ms matters

componentAnnotations.purpose: In-process client that runs the Lua script on Redis
componentAnnotations.technology: Go/Rust library with LRU rule cache
componentAnnotations.notes: Local fallback: in-process counter during Redis outage

componentAnnotations.purpose: Authoritative token bucket state across all gateway instances
componentAnnotations.notes: Atomic Lua script avoids race conditions

componentAnnotations.purpose: Configured limits per identifier type + tier
componentAnnotations.notes: Read-heavy; served from LRU cache in SDK

componentAnnotations.purpose: Push rule changes to SDK instances
Log rejected requests for analytics / abuse team

dataFlowDescription: Hot path: Request → Gateway → SDK.check(id, rule) → Redis EVALSHA (atomic refill + decrement) → allowed/rejected (<1ms p99). On allow, gateway forwards upstream. On reject, gateway returns 429 with headers. Rule changes: Admin UI → Rules DB → emit to Kafka topic → all SDK instances invalidate their LRU entry. Violations: SDK emits fire-and-forget event on each rejection → Kafka → Warehouse for analytics. Redis outage: SDK times out at 5ms → marks instance degraded → falls back to in-process counter with best-effort rate limiting until Redis recovers.

tradeoffs.reason: Token bucket handles bursts naturally (important for latency-sensitive APIs with irregular traffic) at O(1) time and space. Fixed-window has the boundary-effect problem where 2× the limit can be served in the same second across a boundary. Sliding-window is more accurate but costs O(log n) memory per key.

tradeoffs.alternative: Local in-memory counters per gateway instance
tradeoffs.reason: Horizontal gateway scaling requires a shared state; local counters would let each instance independently allow N, so N instances = N×limit effectively. Lua script gives us atomicity without distributed locks.

tradeoffs.alternative: Fail-closed — reject all requests during Redis outage
tradeoffs.reason: Rate limiting is cost-control, not security. Rejecting all traffic during a Redis blip is a much bigger customer-impact incident than letting rate limits briefly not apply. Auth / WAF decisions are independent systems that do fail-closed.

tradeoffs.choice: Per-instance LRU cache of rules + 60s reconciler
tradeoffs.reason: Rules change rarely (seconds to minutes) so caching them in-process is safe. Without the cache, every API request = one Postgres query — unacceptable at 50k QPS.

sources.label: Cloudflare: how we built rate limiting capable of scaling to millions of domains
System Design Interview (Alex Xu), Ch. 4: Rate Limiter
